location_name: Gorah Rocks (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Decapoda, Porifera has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. A broad gully, 10m wide, with coarse sand on bedrock with overlying large slate boulders (to 1.5m). Algae attached to boulders or bedrocks beneath sand. Sand tolerant species in abundance, including Polyides and Furcellaria. Underboulder very rich with near 100% cover by sponges, didemnids and bryozoans, including Diplosoma spongiforme, Trididemnum cereum and Ophlitaspongia seriata. Decapoda well represented as were fish. A rich habitat.

location_name: Langerstone Point (Langerstone Point to Prawle Point)
sample_comment: Lower eulittoral boulder-strewn pools with sand covering, dominated by various red algae. Coralline crusts and Corallina officinalis frequent around the sides of the pool, while Chondus crispus, Furcellaria lunbricalis and Himanthalia elongata were amoung those frequent in the bottom of pools, as was Ulva sp. Sand-tolerant species such as abundant Audouinella and Gracellaria verrucosa were also frequent. Small growths of Cystoseira were also present.

location_name: Langerstone Point (Langerstone Point to Prawle Point)
sample_comment: Rugged lower eulittoral bedrock with dense Mastocarpus stellatus interspersed with himanthalia elongata. Upper faces formrd very flat platforms with abundant and varied algal cover, predominantly reds, with Cladophora sp. and occasional Fucas serratus, with Patella ulyssiponensis and Gibbula umbilicalis. Overhangs held a varied fauna includingGrantia compressa and a variety of sponges, together with frequent Aplidium punctum and Botrylus schlosseri.

location_name: Langerstone Point (Langerstone Point to Prawle Point)
sample_comment: Mid-eulittoral pools in bedrock with afew loose boulders in the bottom. Sides of pools were dominated by Corallinacaea and Corallina officinalis with Mesophyllum lichenoides growing epiphytically. The bottoms of the pools supported a variety of brown algae, including Bifurcaria bifurcata, frequent Halidrs siliquosa and Laminaria digitata. Large tufts of Calliblepharis jubata were the most common red alga. Fuana was sparse, with the pools supporting only anthozoa and an occasional Gibbula umbilicalis.

A broad gully, 10m wide, with coarse sand on bedrock with overlying large slate boulders (to 1.5m). Algae attached to boulders or bedrocks beneath sand. Sand tolerant species in abundance, including Polyides and Furcellaria. Underboulder very rich with near 100% cover by sponges, didemnids and bryozoans, including Diplosoma spongiforme, Trididemnum cereum and Ophlitaspongia seriata. Decapoda well represented as were fish. A rich habitat.

location_name: Gara Rock Bay, East Portlemouth (Gammon Head to The Bull)
sample_comment: Lithophyllum has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Bedrock of shists and igneous intrusions. Large rocky ridges and , near vertical faces with many overhangs, deep pools and fissures. Actina equina, Barnacles. Nucella lapillus and Patella vulgata were found on the exposed faces. Grantia compressa, Halichondria and Hymeniacion were found in shady places and overhangs with Plumaria elegans. Delesseria was found in shady places in deep pools. Opisthobranch were in fissures, as were many Cancer pagarus and Lipophrys pholis. Ridge adjacent to sandy bay and deep gully parallel to shoreline were surveyed.

location_name: Barler Rock (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Porifera indet crusts has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Whole area surveyed. Upper/midshore typical exposed species. Ls deep pools, overhangs and surge gullies very interesting with a diverse flora and fauna of less commonly faunal species such as Halurus equisetifolius, Asparagopsis armata and superabundant Tubularia indivisa in the surge gullies. An excellent site with more to be found on a better tide.

location_name: Lannacombe Beach West (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Porifera has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Rocky plateau with deep pools, fringed by Corallina, and Coralline crusts covering pool sides. Algae growth in pools sparse, often only on Patella. Deep gullies with vertical and overhanging sides harbouring many species in its fissured and creviced surface. Base of gullies scoured with sand/gravel and rock boulder base. More sheltered rockpools were rich in species and high in abundance. Anemones were common, particularly the beadlet. Sponges abundant. The survey reached MLWST, so only just into the Laminaria zone.

location_name: Gorah Rocks (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Shallow pools in a series of steps with occasional runs of water from one level to the next. Near 100% cover by Corallinacea, Actinia equina abundant as were Patella ulyssiponensis with Enteromorpha sp. attached. Bare rocks with sparse Fucus spiralis, Chthamalus and Littorina neritoides common. Ascophyllum nodosum appearing towards bottom of band.

location_name: Lannacombe Bay (west) (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Rhodophycota indet.(non-calc.crusts) has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Hard slate with igneous intrusions forming high (to 5m) ridges, with many overhangs and vertical faces. Exposed seaward sides had much Lithophyllum / Lithothamnion, Corallina spp, Barnacles, Actina equina and Nucella lapilus. On the sheltered sides there were many sponges (Halichondria, Hymeniacidon, Grantia) as well as Polysiphonia spp. and other filamentous reds. A large plateau extended beyond the ridges to LWST. Little rock was visible, being covered in encrusting red algae and Mastocarpus stellatus and kelp. The pools contained few boulders but there was an increased diversity of algae and fauna.

location_name: Lannacombe Bay (east) (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: A rugged bedrock plateau with numerous pools, gullies, overhangs and fissures. Restricted algal species and the majority of those present were not abundnant. Rockpools and gullies contained many boulders and some shelly gravel/sand. Actinia equina were abundant as were the corraline algae. In areas of heavy Patella grazing the Barnacles were dominant, particularly on the open, exposed faces. Richest areas for both species and abundance were the sheltered fissures and the vertical faces/overhangs.

location_name: Gorah Rocks (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Slate and boulder filled gully just below shingle, running down the shore with through flowing water. Algae abundant, particularly Ceramium sp., Ulva and Chondrus etc. Sargassum in pools. Boulders with restricted underboulder community, relatively mobile and dominated by Spirorbidae. Corallinacea abundant, rock surfaces above water with abundant Fucoid and Ascophyllum growth.

location_name: Great Sleaden Rock (Start Point to Little Sleadon Rock)
sample_comment: Pools dominated by corallinaceae with abundant Ceramium spp. and Patella ulyssiponensis. Most pools shallow, deeper pools with Laminaria digitata, Bifurcaria bifucata and Himanthalia elongata. Mytilus spat formed frequent patches in lower pools. Higher pools with few non coralline algae, dominated by coralline crusts and Patella ulyssiponensis.

location_name: Great Sleaden Rock (Start Point to Little Sleadon Rock)
sample_comment: Codium tomentosum has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Red algae dominated rocks with abundant corallinaceae and Balanus perforatus. The low lying rocky shelf was incised by a series of narrow deep surge gullies, scoured by pebble. On severly scoured surfaces, Audouinella sp. and Enteromorpha sp. were locally abundant. Undersurfaces also had a low turf of Lomentaria articulata and Callithamnion tetricum. Codium spp. was present in unusually high quantities. Kelp where present was sparse and grazed by abundant Helcion pellucidum.

location_name: Black Stone, Start Point (Start Point to Little Sleadon Rock)
sample_comment: Lower eulittoral exposed slates dominated by coralline algae, Patella ulyssiponensis and Mastocarpus stellatus. Patches of Laurencia pinnatifida were heavily grazed and vertical faces were dominated by Balanus perforatus. Small clumps of Ceramium spp., Verrucaria mucosa and Callithamnion sp. were frequent.

location_name: Black Stone, Start Point (Start Point to Little Sleadon Rock)
sample_comment: Patella ulyssiponensis has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Shallow gully rockpools in the mid eulittoral were dominated by Corallinaceae and Corallina officinalis with superabundant Patella ulyssiponensis. Fissures held Lipophrys pholis and Sagartia elegans. Limpet shells were covered in Enteromorpha spp.

location_name: Langerstone Point (Langerstone Point to Prawle Point)
sample_comment: Rugged lower eulittoral bedrock with dense Mastocarpus stellatus interspersed with himanthalia elongata. Upper faces formrd very flat platforms with abundant and varied algal cover, predominantly reds, with Cladophora sp. and occasional Fucas serratus, with Patella ulyssiponensis and Gibbula umbilicalis. Overhangs held a varied fauna includingGrantia compressa and a variety of sponges, together with frequent Aplidium punctum and Botrylus schlosseri.

location_name: Langerstone Point (Langerstone Point to Prawle Point)
sample_comment: Lower eulittoral boulder-strewn pools with sand covering, dominated by various red algae. Coralline crusts and Corallina officinalis frequent around the sides of the pool, while Chondus crispus, Furcellaria lunbricalis and Himanthalia elongata were amoung those frequent in the bottom of pools, as was Ulva sp. Sand-tolerant species such as abundant Audouinella and Gracellaria verrucosa were also frequent. Small growths of Cystoseira were also present.

location_name: Langerstone Point (Langerstone Point to Prawle Point)
sample_comment: Mid-eulittoral pools in bedrock with afew loose boulders in the bottom. Sides of pools were dominated by Corallinacaea and Corallina officinalis with Mesophyllum lichenoides growing epiphytically. The bottoms of the pools supported a variety of brown algae, including Bifurcaria bifurcata, frequent Halidrs siliquosa and Laminaria digitata. Large tufts of Calliblepharis jubata were the most common red alga. Fuana was sparse, with the pools supporting only anthozoa and an occasional Gibbula umbilicalis.

location_name: Langerstone Point (Langerstone Point to Prawle Point)
sample_comment: Littorina littorea has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Small, shallow pools in dips in the bedrock. Dominated by Coralline crusts and Corallina officinalis, with frequent Dumontia contorta. Actinia equina were frequent on the sides of the pools, as they were on the surrounding bedrock.

location_name: Gorah Rocks (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Decapoda, Porifera has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. location_name: Lannacombe Beach East (Harris Beach) (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Fucaceae, Nucella, Patella, Trochidae has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Chthamalus montagui has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. The shore consisted of a fairly level, mica shist which contained many fissures and gullies, backed by mud cliffs. Rockpools had been formed in depressions on the surface. The area is characteristic for barnacle cover and encrusting red algae on drying and non-drying surfaces. Seaweed cover was poor, except at the lower shore where encrusting reds were dominant. The shore appeared to be quite exposed from all southerly directions (SW - ESE). This may make it unsuitable for crabs and other large mobile spp. Cthamalus montagui and Corallina in the pools were characteristic as were Patella, Nucella and Topshells.

location_name: Lannacombe (Langerstone Point to Prawle Point)
sample_comment: Exposed flat rocky shelf with many fissures and gullies. Rockpools formed in depressions. The shore is extremely exposed and this is reflected in the species found. Anemones were abundant as was coralline algae.

location_name: Lannacombe Beach (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Cirripedia has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. The upper to mid-shore was a flattish rock plateau with shallow rock pools and gullies. Small amounts of oil residue were found but all the life forms seemed to be healthy. There were large quantities of Corallina to be found and quite a number of Anemonia viridis in evidence. Laurencia was common as were barnacles and limpets. Crabs and starfish were conspicuously absent.

location_name: Lannacombe Bay (east) (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Corallina has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. The rock platform was dissected by gullies running mainly in a SW direction and contained shallow pools with sandy bottoms. Corallina was abundant in the rock pools. The top-mid beach area of incised rock had deep rock pools with cobbles and little else. Many large Thick Topshells , Dogwhelks and eggs were found to be present. Keyhole limpets and cowries were also found. The wide rocky platform extended in both directions from the beach . Mesophyllum lichenoide was found on the corrallina in the shore rockpools.

location_name: Great Sleaden Rock (Start Point to Little Sleadon Rock)
sample_comment: Semibalanus balanoides dominated rock with frequent Balanus perforatus and abundant Patella vulgata. Small red algae on Patella, including Plocamium cartilagineum and Ceramium spp.. Fucus vesiculosus forma linearis was common forming a band from the lower eulittoral up into the lower littoral fringe. Mytilus edulis spat formed a scattered turf.

location_name: Great Sleaden Rock (Start Point to Little Sleadon Rock)
sample_comment: Pools dominated by corallinaceae with abundant Ceramium spp. and Patella ulyssiponensis. Most pools shallow, deeper pools with Laminaria digitata, Bifurcaria bifucata and Himanthalia elongata. Mytilus spat formed frequent patches in lower pools. location_name: Great Sleaden Rock (Start Point to Little Sleadon Rock)
sample_comment: Abundant vertical faces and overhangs of scoured surge gullies and rockmills were dominated by nearly 100% cover of Balanus perforatus. Actinia equina were usually present in large numbers and there was abundant coralline crusts. Halichondria panicea and Hymeniacidon perleve were common, and a variety of red algae, including Lomentaria articulata and Plumaria elegans were frequent.

location_name: Lannacombe (Langerstone Point to Prawle Point)
sample_comment: Exposed flat rocky shelf with many fissures and gullies. Rockpools formed in depressions. The shore is extremely exposed and this is reflected in the species found. Anemones were abundant as was coralline algae.

location_name: Black Stone, Start Point (Start Point to Little Sleadon Rock)
sample_comment: Lower eulittoral exposed slates dominated by coralline algae, Patella ulyssiponensis and Mastocarpus stellatus. Patches of Laurencia pinnatifida were heavily grazed and vertical faces were dominated by Balanus perforatus. Small clumps of Ceramium spp., Verrucaria mucosa and Callithamnion sp. were frequent.

location_name: Black Stone, Start Point (Start Point to Little Sleadon Rock)
sample_comment: Patella ulyssiponensis has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Shallow gully rockpools in the mid eulittoral were dominated by Corallinaceae and Corallina officinalis with superabundant Patella ulyssiponensis. Fissures held Lipophrys pholis and Sagartia elegans. Limpet shells were covered in Enteromorpha spp.

location_name: Langerstone Point (Langerstone Point to Prawle Point)
sample_comment: Rugged lower eulittoral bedrock with dense Mastocarpus stellatus interspersed with himanthalia elongata. Upper faces formrd very flat platforms with abundant and varied algal cover, predominantly reds, with Cladophora sp. and occasional Fucas serratus, with Patella ulyssiponensis and Gibbula umbilicalis. Overhangs held a varied fauna includingGrantia compressa and a variety of sponges, together with frequent Aplidium punctum and Botrylus schlosseri.

location_name: Langerstone Point (Langerstone Point to Prawle Point)
sample_comment: Lower eulittoral boulder-strewn pools with sand covering, dominated by various red algae. Coralline crusts and Corallina officinalis frequent around the sides of the pool, while Chondus crispus, Furcellaria lunbricalis and Himanthalia elongata were amoung those frequent in the bottom of pools, as was Ulva sp. Sand-tolerant species such as abundant Audouinella and Gracellaria verrucosa were also frequent. Small growths of Cystoseira were also present.

location_name: Langerstone Point (Langerstone Point to Prawle Point)
sample_comment: Mid-eulittoral pools in bedrock with afew loose boulders in the bottom. Sides of pools were dominated by Corallinacaea and Corallina officinalis with Mesophyllum lichenoides growing epiphytically. The bottoms of the pools supported a variety of brown algae, including Bifurcaria bifurcata, frequent Halidrs siliquosa and Laminaria digitata. Large tufts of Calliblepharis jubata were the most common red alga. Fuana was sparse, with the pools supporting only anthozoa and an occasional Gibbula umbilicalis.

location_name: Langerstone Point (Langerstone Point to Prawle Point)
sample_comment: Littorina littorea has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Small, shallow pools in dips in the bedrock. Dominated by Coralline crusts and Corallina officinalis, with frequent Dumontia contorta. Actinia equina were frequent on the sides of the pools, as they were on the surrounding bedrock.

location_name: Black Stone, Start Point (Start Point to Little Sleadon Rock)
sample_comment: Lower eulittoral exposed slates dominated by coralline algae, Patella ulyssiponensis and Mastocarpus stellatus. Patches of Laurencia pinnatifida were heavily grazed and vertical faces were dominated by Balanus perforatus. Small clumps of Ceramium spp., Verrucaria mucosa and Callithamnion sp. were frequent.

location_name: Lannacombe Beach East (Harris Beach) (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Fucaceae, Nucella, Patella, Trochidae has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Chthamalus montagui has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. The shore consisted of a fairly level, mica shist which contained many fissures and gullies, backed by mud cliffs. Rockpools had been formed in depressions on the surface. The area is characteristic for barnacle cover and encrusting red algae on drying and non-drying surfaces. Seaweed cover was poor, except at the lower shore where encrusting reds were dominant. The shore appeared to be quite exposed from all southerly directions (SW - ESE). This may make it unsuitable for crabs and other large mobile spp. Cthamalus montagui and Corallina in the pools were characteristic as were Patella, Nucella and Topshells.

location_name: Lannacombe Bay (east) (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: A rugged bedrock plateau with numerous pools, gullies, overhangs and fissures. Restricted algal species and the majority of those present were not abundnant. Rockpools and gullies contained many boulders and some shelly gravel/sand. Actinia equina were abundant as were the corraline algae. In areas of heavy Patella grazing the Barnacles were dominant, particularly on the open, exposed faces. Richest areas for both species and abundance were the sheltered fissures and the vertical faces/overhangs.

location_name: Barler Rock (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Porifera indet crusts has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Whole area surveyed. Upper/midshore typical exposed species. Ls deep pools, overhangs and surge gullies very interesting with a diverse flora and fauna of less commonly faunal species such as Halurus equisetifolius, Asparagopsis armata and superabundant Tubularia indivisa in the surge gullies. An excellent site with more to be found on a better tide.

location_name: Gorah Rocks (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Slate and boulder filled gully just below shingle, running down the shore with through flowing water. Algae abundant, particularly Ceramium sp., Ulva and Chondrus etc. Sargassum in pools. Boulders with restricted underboulder community, relatively mobile and dominated by Spirorbidae. Corallinacea abundant, rock surfaces above water with abundant Fucoid and Ascophyllum growth.

location_name: Gorah Rocks (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Decapoda, Porifera has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. A broad gully, 10m wide, with coarse sand on bedrock with overlying large slate boulders (to 1.5m). Algae attached to boulders or bedrocks beneath sand. Sand tolerant species in abundance, including Polyides and Furcellaria. Underboulder very rich with near 100% cover by sponges, didemnids and bryozoans, including Diplosoma spongiforme, Trididemnum cereum and Ophlitaspongia seriata. Decapoda well represented as were fish. A rich habitat.

location_name: Lannacombe (Langerstone Point to Prawle Point)
sample_comment: Exposed flat rocky shelf with many fissures and gullies. Rockpools formed in depressions. The shore is extremely exposed and this is reflected in the species found. Anemones were abundant as was coralline algae.

location_name: Lannacombe Beach (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Cirripedia has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. The upper to mid-shore was a flattish rock plateau with shallow rock pools and gullies. Small amounts of oil residue were found but all the life forms seemed to be healthy. There were large quantities of Corallina to be found and quite a number of Anemonia viridis in evidence. Laurencia was common as were barnacles and limpets. Crabs and starfish were conspicuously absent.

location_name: Lannacombe Beach East (Harris Beach) (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Fucaceae, Nucella, Patella, Trochidae has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. location_name: Gara Rock Bay, East Portlemouth (Gammon Head to The Bull)
sample_comment: Lithophyllum has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Bedrock of shists and igneous intrusions. Large rocky ridges and , near vertical faces with many overhangs, deep pools and fissures. Actina equina, Barnacles. Nucella lapillus and Patella vulgata were found on the exposed faces. Grantia compressa, Halichondria and Hymeniacion were found in shady places and overhangs with Plumaria elegans. Delesseria was found in shady places in deep pools. Opisthobranch were in fissures, as were many Cancer pagarus and Lipophrys pholis. Ridge adjacent to sandy bay and deep gully parallel to shoreline were surveyed.

location_name: Lannacombe Bay (west) (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Rhodophycota indet.(non-calc.crusts) has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Hard slate with igneous intrusions forming high (to 5m) ridges, with many overhangs and vertical faces. Exposed seaward sides had much Lithophyllum / Lithothamnion, Corallina spp, Barnacles, Actina equina and Nucella lapilus. On the sheltered sides there were many sponges (Halichondria, Hymeniacidon, Grantia) as well as Polysiphonia spp. and other filamentous reds. A large plateau extended beyond the ridges to LWST. Little rock was visible, being covered in encrusting red algae and Mastocarpus stellatus and kelp. The pools contained few boulders but there was an increased diversity of algae and fauna.

location_name: Lannacombe Beach West (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: The shore consisted of a rocky platform with many gullies, rockpools, fissures and crevices. A large variety of species were found.

location_name: Lannacombe Beach West (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Porifera has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Rocky plateau with deep pools, fringed by Corallina, and Coralline crusts covering pool sides. Algae growth in pools sparse, often only on Patella. Deep gullies with vertical and overhanging sides harbouring many species in its fissured and creviced surface. Base of gullies scoured with sand/gravel and rock boulder base. More sheltered rockpools were rich in species and high in abundance. Anemones were common, particularly the beadlet. Sponges abundant. The survey reached MLWST, so only just into the Laminaria zone.

location_name: Gorah Rocks (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Shallow pools in a series of steps with occasional runs of water from one level to the next. Near 100% cover by Corallinacea, Actinia equina abundant as were Patella ulyssiponensis with Enteromorpha sp. attached. Bare rocks with sparse Fucus spiralis, Chthamalus and Littorina neritoides common. Ascophyllum nodosum appearing towards bottom of band.

sample_comment: Corallina has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. The rock platform was dissected by gullies running mainly in a SW direction and contained shallow pools with sandy bottoms. Corallina was abundant in the rock pools. The top-mid beach area of incised rock had deep rock pools with cobbles and little else. Many large Thick Topshells , Dogwhelks and eggs were found to be present. Codium spp. was present in unusually high quantities. Kelp where present was sparse and grazed by abundant Helcion pellucidum.

location_name: West Lannacombe Beach (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Very exposed shore consisting of a rock platform with gullies and rockpools. A large variety of species was found and the area was abundant in oarweed. The survey was incomplete.

location_name: Lannacombe Beach West (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: The shore consisted of a rocky platform with many gullies, rockpools, fissures and crevices. A large variety of species were found.

location_name: Lannacombe (Langerstone Point to Prawle Point)
sample_comment: Exposed flat rocky shelf with many fissures and gullies. Rockpools formed in depressions. The shore is extremely exposed and this is reflected in the species found. Anemones were abundant as was coralline algae.

location_name: Gorah Rocks (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Decapoda, Porifera has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. A broad gully, 10m wide, with coarse sand on bedrock with overlying large slate boulders (to 1.5m). Algae attached to boulders or bedrocks beneath sand. Sand tolerant species in abundance, including Polyides and Furcellaria. Underboulder very rich with near 100% cover by sponges, didemnids and bryozoans, including Diplosoma spongiforme, Trididemnum cereum and Ophlitaspongia seriata. Decapoda well represented as were fish. A rich habitat.

location_name: Black Stone, Start Point (Start Point to Little Sleadon Rock)
sample_comment: Littorininae has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Rugged infralittoral slates dominated by Porphyra umbilicalis on Verrucaria maura and scattered Chthamalus spp. Damp patches and crevices supported littorinids and Hildenbrandia sp. Small pools held Tigriopus brevicornis.

location_name: Langerstone Point (Langerstone Point to Prawle Point)
sample_comment: Exposed bedrock in the littoral fringe with sparse patches of Pelvetia canaliculata. Verrucaria maura was superabundant, completely covering much of this zone, broken only by scattered tufts of Lichina pygmaea, with occasional barnacles, (Chthamalus montagui) and Patella vulgata. Small shallow pools were dominated by Enteromorpha sp. Supralittoral recorded seperately.

location_name: Langerstone Point (Langerstone Point to Prawle Point)
sample_comment: Supralittoral, predominatly upward facing bedrock, dominated by Caloplaca sp. Lecanora atra and indeterminate grey lickens. Ramalina sp. was abundant, particuarly growing out of fissures. There were several pools in this area, comepltely dominated by Enteromorpha.

location_name: Lannacombe Beach West (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Porifera has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Rocky plateau with deep pools, fringed by Corallina, and Coralline crusts covering pool sides. Algae growth in pools sparse, often only on Patella. Deep gullies with vertical and overhanging sides harbouring many species in its fissured and creviced surface. Base of gullies scoured with sand/gravel and rock boulder base. More sheltered rockpools were rich in species and high in abundance. Anemones were common, particularly the beadlet. Sponges abundant. The survey reached MLWST, so only just into the Laminaria zone.

location_name: Gara Rock Bay, East Portlemouth (Gammon Head to The Bull)
sample_comment: Lithophyllum has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Bedrock of shists and igneous intrusions. Large rocky ridges and , near vertical faces with many overhangs, deep pools and fissures. Actina equina, Barnacles. Nucella lapillus and Patella vulgata were found on the exposed faces. Grantia compressa, Halichondria and Hymeniacion were found in shady places and overhangs with Plumaria elegans. Delesseria was found in shady places in deep pools. Opisthobranch were in fissures, as were many Cancer pagarus and Lipophrys pholis. Ridge adjacent to sandy bay and deep gully parallel to shoreline were surveyed.

location_name: Lannacombe Beach (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Cirripedia has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. The upper to mid-shore was a flattish rock plateau with shallow rock pools and gullies. Small amounts of oil residue were found but all the life forms seemed to be healthy. There were large quantities of Corallina to be found and quite a number of Anemonia viridis in evidence. Laurencia was common as were barnacles and limpets. Crabs and starfish were conspicuously absent.

location_name: Lannacombe Bay (east) (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Corallina has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. The rock platform was dissected by gullies running mainly in a SW direction and contained shallow pools with sandy bottoms. Corallina was abundant in the rock pools. The top-mid beach area of incised rock had deep rock pools with cobbles and little else. Many large Thick Topshells , Dogwhelks and eggs were found to be present. Keyhole limpets and cowries were also found. The wide rocky platform extended in both directions from the beach . Mesophyllum lichenoide was found on the corrallina in the shore rockpools.

location_name: Lannacombe Bay (west) (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Rhodophycota indet.(non-calc.crusts) has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Hard slate with igneous intrusions forming high (to 5m) ridges, with many overhangs and vertical faces. Exposed seaward sides had much Lithophyllum / Lithothamnion, Corallina spp, Barnacles, Actina equina and Nucella lapilus. On the sheltered sides there were many sponges (Halichondria, Hymeniacidon, Grantia) as well as Polysiphonia spp. and other filamentous reds. A large plateau extended beyond the ridges to LWST. Little rock was visible, being covered in encrusting red algae and Mastocarpus stellatus and kelp. The pools contained few boulders but there was an increased diversity of algae and fauna.

location_name: Lannacombe Bay (east) (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: A rugged bedrock plateau with numerous pools, gullies, overhangs and fissures. Restricted algal species and the majority of those present were not abundnant. Rockpools and gullies contained many boulders and some shelly gravel/sand. Actinia equina were abundant as were the corraline algae. In areas of heavy Patella grazing the Barnacles were dominant, particularly on the open, exposed faces. Richest areas for both species and abundance were the sheltered fissures and the vertical faces/overhangs.

location_name: Barler Rock (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Porifera indet crusts has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Whole area surveyed. Upper/midshore typical exposed species. Ls deep pools, overhangs and surge gullies very interesting with a diverse flora and fauna of less commonly faunal species such as Halurus equisetifolius, Asparagopsis armata and superabundant Tubularia indivisa in the surge gullies. An excellent site with more to be found on a better tide.

location_name: Great Sleaden Rock (Start Point to Little Sleadon Rock)
sample_comment: Pools dominated by corallinaceae with abundant Ceramium spp. and Patella ulyssiponensis. Most pools shallow, deeper pools with Laminaria digitata, Bifurcaria bifucata and Himanthalia elongata. Mytilus spat formed frequent patches in lower pools. Higher pools with few non coralline algae, dominated by coralline crusts and Patella ulyssiponensis.

location_name: Langerstone Point (Langerstone Point to Prawle Point)
sample_comment: Mid-eulittoral pools in bedrock with afew loose boulders in the bottom. Sides of pools were dominated by Corallinacaea and Corallina officinalis with Mesophyllum lichenoides growing epiphytically. The bottoms of the pools supported a variety of brown algae, including Bifurcaria bifurcata, frequent Halidrs siliquosa and Laminaria digitata. Large tufts of Calliblepharis jubata were the most common red alga. Fuana was sparse, with the pools supporting only anthozoa and an occasional Gibbula umbilicalis.

location_name: Langerstone Point (Langerstone Point to Prawle Point)
sample_comment: Rugged lower eulittoral bedrock with dense Mastocarpus stellatus interspersed with himanthalia elongata. Upper faces formrd very flat platforms with abundant and varied algal cover, predominantly reds, with Cladophora sp. and occasional Fucas serratus, with Patella ulyssiponensis and Gibbula umbilicalis. Overhangs held a varied fauna includingGrantia compressa and a variety of sponges, together with frequent Aplidium punctum and Botrylus schlosseri.

location_name: Langerstone Point (Langerstone Point to Prawle Point)
sample_comment: Rugged lower eulittoral bedrock with dense Mastocarpus stellatus interspersed with himanthalia elongata. location_name: Lannacombe Beach East (Harris Beach) (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Fucaceae, Nucella, Patella, Trochidae has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Chthamalus montagui has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. The shore consisted of a fairly level, mica shist which contained many fissures and gullies, backed by mud cliffs. Rockpools had been formed in depressions on the surface. The area is characteristic for barnacle cover and encrusting red algae on drying and non-drying surfaces. Seaweed cover was poor, except at the lower shore where encrusting reds were dominant. The shore appeared to be quite exposed from all southerly directions (SW - ESE). This may make it unsuitable for crabs and other large mobile spp. Cthamalus montagui and Corallina in the pools were characteristic as were Patella, Nucella and Topshells.

location_name: Barler Rock (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Porifera indet crusts has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Whole area surveyed. An excellent site with more to be found on a better tide.

location_name: Gara Rock Bay, East Portlemouth (Gammon Head to The Bull)
sample_comment: Lithophyllum has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Bedrock of shists and igneous intrusions. Large rocky ridges and , near vertical faces with many overhangs, deep pools and fissures. Actina equina, Barnacles. Nucella lapillus and Patella vulgata were found on the exposed faces. Grantia compressa, Halichondria and Hymeniacion were found in shady places and overhangs with Plumaria elegans. Delesseria was found in shady places in deep pools. Opisthobranch were in fissures, as were many Cancer pagarus and Lipophrys pholis. Ridge adjacent to sandy bay and deep gully parallel to shoreline were surveyed.

location_name: Lannacombe Bay (east) (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Corallina has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. The rock platform was dissected by gullies running mainly in a SW direction and contained shallow pools with sandy bottoms. Corallina was abundant in the rock pools. The top-mid beach area of incised rock had deep rock pools with cobbles and little else. Many large Thick Topshells , Dogwhelks and eggs were found to be present. Keyhole limpets and cowries were also found. The wide rocky platform extended in both directions from the beach . Mesophyllum lichenoide was found on the corrallina in the shore rockpools.

location_name: Lannacombe Bay (west) (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Rhodophycota indet.(non-calc.crusts) has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Hard slate with igneous intrusions forming high (to 5m) ridges, with many overhangs and vertical faces. Exposed seaward sides had much Lithophyllum / Lithothamnion, Corallina spp, Barnacles, Actina equina and Nucella lapilus. On the sheltered sides there were many sponges (Halichondria, Hymeniacidon, Grantia) as well as Polysiphonia spp. and other filamentous reds. A large plateau extended beyond the ridges to LWST. Little rock was visible, being covered in encrusting red algae and Mastocarpus stellatus and kelp. The pools contained few boulders but there was an increased diversity of algae and fauna.

location_name: Lannacombe Bay (east) (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: A rugged bedrock plateau with numerous pools, gullies, overhangs and fissures. Restricted algal species and the majority of those present were not abundnant. Rockpools and gullies contained many boulders and some shelly gravel/sand. Actinia equina were abundant as were the corraline algae. In areas of heavy Patella grazing the Barnacles were dominant, particularly on the open, exposed faces. Richest areas for both species and abundance were the sheltered fissures and the vertical faces/overhangs.

location_name: Great Sleaden Rock (Start Point to Little Sleadon Rock)

location_name: Great Sleaden Rock (Start Point to Little Sleadon Rock)
sample_comment: Pools dominated by corallinaceae with abundant Ceramium spp. and Patella ulyssiponensis. Most pools shallow, deeper pools with Laminaria digitata, Bifurcaria bifucata and Himanthalia elongata. Mytilus spat formed frequent patches in lower pools. Higher pools with few non coralline algae, dominated by coralline crusts and Patella ulyssiponensis.

location_name: Great Sleaden Rock (Start Point to Little Sleadon Rock)
sample_comment: Codium tomentosum has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. On severly scoured surfaces, Audouinella sp. and Enteromorpha sp. were locally abundant. Undersurfaces also had a low turf of Lomentaria articulata and Callithamnion tetricum. Codium spp. was present in unusually high quantities. Kelp where present was sparse and grazed by abundant Helcion pellucidum.

Near 100% cover by Corallinacea, Actinia equina abundant as were Patella ulyssiponensis with Enteromorpha sp. attached. location_name: Gorah Rocks (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Slate and boulder filled gully just below shingle, running down the shore with through flowing water. Algae abundant, particularly Ceramium sp., Ulva and Chondrus etc. Sargassum in pools. Boulders with restricted underboulder community, relatively mobile and dominated by Spirorbidae. Corallinacea abundant, rock surfaces above water with abundant Fucoid and Ascophyllum growth.

location_name: Langerstone Point (Langerstone Point to Prawle Point)
sample_comment: Lower eulittoral boulder-strewn pools with sand covering, dominated by various red algae. Coralline crusts and Corallina officinalis frequent around the sides of the pool, while Chondus crispus, Furcellaria lunbricalis and Himanthalia elongata were amoung those frequent in the bottom of pools, as was Ulva sp. Sand-tolerant species such as abundant Audouinella and Gracellaria verrucosa were also frequent. Small growths of Cystoseira were also present.

location_name: Langerstone Point (Langerstone Point to Prawle Point)
sample_comment: Mid-eulittoral pools in bedrock with afew loose boulders in the bottom. Sides of pools were dominated by Corallinacaea and Corallina officinalis with Mesophyllum lichenoides growing epiphytically. The bottoms of the pools supported a variety of brown algae, including Bifurcaria bifurcata, frequent Halidrs siliquosa and Laminaria digitata. Large tufts of Calliblepharis jubata were the most common red alga. Fuana was sparse, with the pools supporting only anthozoa and an occasional Gibbula umbilicalis.

Hard slate with igneous intrusions forming high (to 5m) ridges, with many overhangs and vertical faces. Exposed seaward sides had much Lithophyllum / Lithothamnion, Corallina spp, Barnacles, Actina equina and Nucella lapilus. On the sheltered sides there were many sponges (Halichondria, Hymeniacidon, Grantia) as well as Polysiphonia spp. and other filamentous reds. A large plateau extended beyond the ridges to LWST. Little rock was visible, being covered in encrusting red algae and Mastocarpus stellatus and kelp. The pools contained few boulders but there was an increased diversity of algae and fauna.

location_name: West Lannacombe Beach (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Very exposed shore consisting of a rock platform with gullies and rockpools. A large variety of species was found and the area was abundant in oarweed. The survey was incomplete.

location_name: Barler Rock (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Porifera indet crusts has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Whole area surveyed. Upper/midshore typical exposed species. Ls deep pools, overhangs and surge gullies very interesting with a diverse flora and fauna of less commonly faunal species such as Halurus equisetifolius, Asparagopsis armata and superabundant Tubularia indivisa in the surge gullies. An excellent site with more to be found on a better tide.

location_name: Lannacombe Beach West (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: The shore consisted of a rocky platform with many gullies, rockpools, fissures and crevices. A large variety of species were found.

location_name: Lannacombe Beach West (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Porifera has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Rocky plateau with deep pools, fringed by Corallina, and Coralline crusts covering pool sides. Algae growth in pools sparse, often only on Patella. Deep gullies with vertical and overhanging sides harbouring many species in its fissured and creviced surface. Base of gullies scoured with sand/gravel and rock boulder base. More sheltered rockpools were rich in species and high in abundance. Anemones were common, particularly the beadlet. Sponges abundant. The survey reached MLWST, so only just into the Laminaria zone.

location_name: Gorah Rocks (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Slate and boulder filled gully just below shingle, running down the shore with through flowing water. Algae abundant, particularly Ceramium sp., Ulva and Chondrus etc. Sargassum in pools. Boulders with restricted underboulder community, relatively mobile and dominated by Spirorbidae. Corallinacea abundant, rock surfaces above water with abundant Fucoid and Ascophyllum growth.

location_name: Gorah Rocks (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Decapoda, Porifera has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. A broad gully, 10m wide, with coarse sand on bedrock with overlying large slate boulders (to 1.5m). Algae attached to boulders or bedrocks beneath sand. Sand tolerant species in abundance, including Polyides and Furcellaria. Underboulder very rich with near 100% cover by sponges, didemnids and bryozoans, including Diplosoma spongiforme, Trididemnum cereum and Ophlitaspongia seriata. Decapoda well represented as were fish. A rich habitat.

location_name: Lannacombe Bay (east) (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: A rugged bedrock plateau with numerous pools, gullies, overhangs and fissures. Restricted algal species and the majority of those present were not abundnant. Rockpools and gullies contained many boulders and some shelly gravel/sand. Actinia equina were abundant as were the corraline algae. In areas of heavy Patella grazing the Barnacles were dominant, particularly on the open, exposed faces. Richest areas for both species and abundance were the sheltered fissures and the vertical faces/overhangs.

location_name: Barler Rock (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Porifera indet crusts has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Whole area surveyed. Upper/midshore typical exposed species. Ls deep pools, overhangs and surge gullies very interesting with a diverse flora and fauna of less commonly faunal species such as Halurus equisetifolius, Asparagopsis armata and superabundant Tubularia indivisa in the surge gullies. An excellent site with more to be found on a better tide.

location_name: Lannacombe Beach West (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Porifera has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Rocky plateau with deep pools, fringed by Corallina, and Coralline crusts covering pool sides. Algae growth in pools sparse, often only on Patella. location_name: Gorah Rocks (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Ramalina siliquosa has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. One of a few rock outcrops which reach through the uppershore sand and shingle to reach the cliff base at 6-7m ACD. Lichen dominated though not particularly well covered, Ramalina siliquosa short and stubby. Green lichens occasional.

location_name: Great Sleaden Rock (Start Point to Little Sleadon Rock)
sample_comment: Ramalina siliquosa, Caloplaca has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Supralittoral lichens were noted from over 40 metres above the shore, particularly Xanthoria parietina and Ramalina siliquosa, though only the 4-8m ASL band was surveyed. Abundance was high though diversity was not great. Grey lichens abundant.

location_name: Langerstone Point (Langerstone Point to Prawle Point)
sample_comment: Supralittoral, predominatly upward facing bedrock, dominated by Caloplaca sp. Lecanora atra and indeterminate grey lickens. Ramalina sp. was abundant, particuarly growing out of fissures. There were several pools in this area, comepltely dominated by Enteromorpha.

location_name: Lannacombe Beach East (Harris Beach) (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Fucaceae, Nucella, Patella, Trochidae has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Chthamalus montagui has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. The shore consisted of a fairly level, mica shist which contained many fissures and gullies, backed by mud cliffs. Rockpools had been formed in depressions on the surface. The area is characteristic for barnacle cover and encrusting red algae on drying and non-drying surfaces. Seaweed cover was poor, except at the lower shore where encrusting reds were dominant. The shore appeared to be quite exposed from all southerly directions (SW - ESE). This may make it unsuitable for crabs and other large mobile spp. Cthamalus montagui and Corallina in the pools were characteristic as were Patella, Nucella and Topshells.

location_name: Gara Rock Bay, East Portlemouth (Gammon Head to The Bull)
sample_comment: Lithophyllum has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Bedrock of shists and igneous intrusions. Large rocky ridges and , near vertical faces with many overhangs, deep pools and fissures. Actina equina, Barnacles. Nucella lapillus and Patella vulgata were found on the exposed faces. Grantia compressa, Halichondria and Hymeniacion were found in shady places and overhangs with Plumaria elegans. Delesseria was found in shady places in deep pools. Opisthobranch were in fissures, as were many Cancer pagarus and Lipophrys pholis. Ridge adjacent to sandy bay and deep gully parallel to shoreline were surveyed.

location_name: Lannacombe Beach West (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Porifera has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. location_name: Lannacombe (Langerstone Point to Prawle Point)
sample_comment: Exposed flat rocky shelf with many fissures and gullies. Rockpools formed in depressions. The shore is extremely exposed and this is reflected in the species found. Anemones were abundant as was coralline algae.

location_name: Black Stone, Start Point (Start Point to Little Sleadon Rock)
sample_comment: Lower eulittoral exposed slates dominated by coralline algae, Patella ulyssiponensis and Mastocarpus stellatus. Patches of Laurencia pinnatifida were heavily grazed and vertical faces were dominated by Balanus perforatus. Small clumps of Ceramium spp., Verrucaria mucosa and Callithamnion sp. were frequent.

location_name: Black Stone, Start Point (Start Point to Little Sleadon Rock)
sample_comment: Patella ulyssiponensis has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Shallow gully rockpools in the mid eulittoral were dominated by Corallinaceae and Corallina officinalis with superabundant Patella ulyssiponensis. Fissures held Lipophrys pholis and Sagartia elegans. Limpet shells were covered in Enteromorpha spp.

location_name: Lannacombe Bay (east) (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Corallina has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. The rock platform was dissected by gullies running mainly in a SW direction and contained shallow pools with sandy bottoms. Corallina was abundant in the rock pools. The top-mid beach area of incised rock had deep rock pools with cobbles and little else. Many large Thick Topshells , Dogwhelks and eggs were found to be present. Keyhole limpets and cowries were also found. The wide rocky platform extended in both directions from the beach . Mesophyllum lichenoide was found on the corrallina in the shore rockpools.

location_name: Langerstone Point (Langerstone Point to Prawle Point)
sample_comment: Rugged lower eulittoral bedrock with dense Mastocarpus stellatus interspersed with himanthalia elongata. Upper faces formrd very flat platforms with abundant and varied algal cover, predominantly reds, with Cladophora sp. and occasional Fucas serratus, with Patella ulyssiponensis and Gibbula umbilicalis. Overhangs held a varied fauna includingGrantia compressa and a variety of sponges, together with frequent Aplidium punctum and Botrylus schlosseri.

location_name: Lannacombe Beach (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Cirripedia has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. The upper to mid-shore was a flattish rock plateau with shallow rock pools and gullies. Small amounts of oil residue were found but all the life forms seemed to be healthy. There were large quantities of Corallina to be found and quite a number of Anemonia viridis in evidence. Laurencia was common as were barnacles and limpets. Crabs and starfish were conspicuously absent.

location_name: Great Sleaden Rock (Start Point to Little Sleadon Rock)
sample_comment: Semibalanus balanoides dominated rock with frequent Balanus perforatus and abundant Patella vulgata. Small red algae on Patella, including Plocamium cartilagineum and Ceramium spp.. Fucus vesiculosus forma linearis was common forming a band from the lower eulittoral up into the lower littoral fringe. Mytilus edulis spat formed a scattered turf.

location_name: Great Sleaden Rock (Start Point to Little Sleadon Rock)
sample_comment: Pools dominated by corallinaceae with abundant Ceramium spp. and Patella ulyssiponensis. Most pools shallow, deeper pools with Laminaria digitata, Bifurcaria bifucata and Himanthalia elongata. Mytilus spat formed frequent patches in lower pools. Higher pools with few non coralline algae, dominated by coralline crusts and Patella ulyssiponensis.

location_name: Great Sleaden Rock (Start Point to Little Sleadon Rock)
sample_comment: Codium tomentosum has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Red algae dominated rocks with abundant corallinaceae and Balanus perforatus. The low lying rocky shelf was incised by a series of narrow deep surge gullies, scoured by pebble. On severly scoured surfaces, Audouinella sp. and Enteromorpha sp. were locally abundant. Undersurfaces also had a low turf of Lomentaria articulata and Callithamnion tetricum. Codium spp. was present in unusually high quantities. Kelp where present was sparse and grazed by abundant Helcion pellucidum.

location_name: Great Sleaden Rock (Start Point to Little Sleadon Rock)
sample_comment: Abundant vertical faces and overhangs of scoured surge gullies and rockmills were dominated by nearly 100% cover of Balanus perforatus. Actinia equina were usually present in large numbers and there was abundant coralline crusts. Halichondria panicea and Hymeniacidon perleve were common, and a variety of red algae, including Lomentaria articulata and Plumaria elegans were frequent.

location_name: Lannacombe (Langerstone Point to Prawle Point)
sample_comment: Exposed flat rocky shelf with many fissures and gullies. Rockpools formed in depressions. The shore is extremely exposed and this is reflected in the species found. Anemones were abundant as was coralline algae.

location_name: Langerstone Point (Langerstone Point to Prawle Point)
sample_comment: Rugged lower eulittoral bedrock with dense Mastocarpus stellatus interspersed with himanthalia elongata. Upper faces formrd very flat platforms with abundant and varied algal cover, predominantly reds, with Cladophora sp. and occasional Fucas serratus, with Patella ulyssiponensis and Gibbula umbilicalis. Overhangs held a varied fauna includingGrantia compressa and a variety of sponges, together with frequent Aplidium punctum and Botrylus schlosseri.

location_name: Langerstone Point (Langerstone Point to Prawle Point)
sample_comment: Lower eulittoral boulder-strewn pools with sand covering, dominated by various red algae. Coralline crusts and Corallina officinalis frequent around the sides of the pool, while Chondus crispus, Furcellaria lunbricalis and Himanthalia elongata were amoung those frequent in the bottom of pools, as was Ulva sp. Sand-tolerant species such as abundant Audouinella and Gracellaria verrucosa were also frequent. Small growths of Cystoseira were also present.

location_name: Langerstone Point (Langerstone Point to Prawle Point)
sample_comment: Mid-eulittoral pools in bedrock with afew loose boulders in the bottom. Sides of pools were dominated by Corallinacaea and Corallina officinalis with Mesophyllum lichenoides growing epiphytically. The bottoms of the pools supported a variety of brown algae, including Bifurcaria bifurcata, frequent Halidrs siliquosa and Laminaria digitata. Large tufts of Calliblepharis jubata were the most common red alga. Fuana was sparse, with the pools supporting only anthozoa and an occasional Gibbula umbilicalis.

location_name: Gorah Rocks (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Decapoda, Porifera has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. A broad gully, 10m wide, with coarse sand on bedrock with overlying large slate boulders (to 1.5m). Algae attached to boulders or bedrocks beneath sand. Sand tolerant species in abundance, including Polyides and Furcellaria. Underboulder very rich with near 100% cover by sponges, didemnids and bryozoans, including Diplosoma spongiforme, Trididemnum cereum and Ophlitaspongia seriata. Decapoda well represented as were fish. A rich habitat.

location_name: Langerstone Point (Langerstone Point to Prawle Point)
sample_comment: Lower eulittoral boulder-strewn pools with sand covering, dominated by various red algae. Coralline crusts and Corallina officinalis frequent around the sides of the pool, while Chondus crispus, Furcellaria lunbricalis and Himanthalia elongata were amoung those frequent in the bottom of pools, as was Ulva sp. Sand-tolerant species such as abundant Audouinella and Gracellaria verrucosa were also frequent. Small growths of Cystoseira were also present.

location_name: Lannacombe Beach West (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Porifera has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Rocky plateau with deep pools, fringed by Corallina, and Coralline crusts covering pool sides. Algae growth in pools sparse, often only on Patella. Deep gullies with vertical and overhanging sides harbouring many species in its fissured and creviced surface. Base of gullies scoured with sand/gravel and rock boulder base. More sheltered rockpools were rich in species and high in abundance. Anemones were common, particularly the beadlet. Sponges abundant. The survey reached MLWST, so only just into the Laminaria zone.

Nucella lapillus and Patella vulgata were found on the exposed faces. Grantia compressa, Halichondria and Hymeniacion were found in shady places and overhangs with Plumaria elegans. Delesseria was found in shady places in deep pools. Opisthobranch were in fissures, as were many Cancer pagarus and Lipophrys pholis. Ridge adjacent to sandy bay and deep gully parallel to shoreline were surveyed.

location_name: Barler Rock (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Porifera indet crusts has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Whole area surveyed. Upper/midshore typical exposed species. Ls deep pools, overhangs and surge gullies very interesting with a diverse flora and fauna of less commonly faunal species such as Halurus equisetifolius, Asparagopsis armata and superabundant Tubularia indivisa in the surge gullies. An excellent site with more to be found on a better tide.

location_name: Gorah Rocks (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Shallow pools in a series of steps with occasional runs of water from one level to the next. Near 100% cover by Corallinacea, Actinia equina abundant as were Patella ulyssiponensis with Enteromorpha sp. attached. Bare rocks with sparse Fucus spiralis, Chthamalus and Littorina neritoides common. Ascophyllum nodosum appearing towards bottom of band.

location_name: Lannacombe Bay (west) (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Rhodophycota indet.(non-calc.crusts) has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Hard slate with igneous intrusions forming high (to 5m) ridges, with many overhangs and vertical faces. Exposed seaward sides had much Lithophyllum / Lithothamnion, Corallina spp, Barnacles, Actina equina and Nucella lapilus. On the sheltered sides there were many sponges (Halichondria, Hymeniacidon, Grantia) as well as Polysiphonia spp. and other filamentous reds. A large plateau extended beyond the ridges to LWST. Little rock was visible, being covered in encrusting red algae and Mastocarpus stellatus and kelp. The pools contained few boulders but there was an increased diversity of algae and fauna.

location_name: Lannacombe Bay (east) (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: A rugged bedrock plateau with numerous pools, gullies, overhangs and fissures. Restricted algal species and the majority of those present were not abundnant. Rockpools and gullies contained many boulders and some shelly gravel/sand. Actinia equina were abundant as were the corraline algae. In areas of heavy Patella grazing the Barnacles were dominant, particularly on the open, exposed faces. Richest areas for both species and abundance were the sheltered fissures and the vertical faces/overhangs.

location_name: Gorah Rocks (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Ramalina siliquosa has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. One of a few rock outcrops which reach through the uppershore sand and shingle to reach the cliff base at 6-7m ACD. Lichen dominated though not particularly well covered, Ramalina siliquosa short and stubby. Green lichens occasional.

location_name: Great Sleaden Rock (Start Point to Little Sleadon Rock)
sample_comment: Ramalina siliquosa, Caloplaca has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Supralittoral lichens were noted from over 40 metres above the shore, particularly Xanthoria parietina and Ramalina siliquosa, though only the 4-8m ASL band was surveyed. Abundance was high though diversity was not great. Grey lichens abundant.

location_name: Lannacombe (Langerstone Point to Prawle Point)
sample_comment: Exposed flat rocky shelf with many fissures and gullies. Rockpools formed in depressions. The shore is extremely exposed and this is reflected in the species found. Anemones were abundant as was coralline algae.

location_name: Langerstone Point (Langerstone Point to Prawle Point)
sample_comment: Supralittoral, predominatly upward facing bedrock, dominated by Caloplaca sp. Lecanora atra and indeterminate grey lickens. Ramalina sp. was abundant, particuarly growing out of fissures. There were several pools in this area, comepltely dominated by Enteromorpha.

location_name: Black Stone, Start Point (Start Point to Little Sleadon Rock)
sample_comment: Lower eulittoral exposed slates dominated by coralline algae, Patella ulyssiponensis and Mastocarpus stellatus. Patches of Laurencia pinnatifida were heavily grazed and vertical faces were dominated by Balanus perforatus. Small clumps of Ceramium spp., Verrucaria mucosa and Callithamnion sp. were frequent.

location_name: Langerstone Point (Langerstone Point to Prawle Point)
sample_comment: Rugged lower eulittoral bedrock with dense Mastocarpus stellatus interspersed with himanthalia elongata. Upper faces formrd very flat platforms with abundant and varied algal cover, predominantly reds, with Cladophora sp. and occasional Fucas serratus, with Patella ulyssiponensis and Gibbula umbilicalis. Overhangs held a varied fauna includingGrantia compressa and a variety of sponges, together with frequent Aplidium punctum and Botrylus schlosseri.

location_name: Lannacombe Beach (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Cirripedia has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. The upper to mid-shore was a flattish rock plateau with shallow rock pools and gullies. Small amounts of oil residue were found but all the life forms seemed to be healthy. There were large quantities of Corallina to be found and quite a number of Anemonia viridis in evidence. Laurencia was common as were barnacles and limpets. Crabs and starfish were conspicuously absent.

location_name: Lannacombe Beach East (Harris Beach) (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Fucaceae, Nucella, Patella, Trochidae has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Chthamalus montagui has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. The shore consisted of a fairly level, mica shist which contained many fissures and gullies, backed by mud cliffs. Rockpools had been formed in depressions on the surface. The area is characteristic for barnacle cover and encrusting red algae on drying and non-drying surfaces. Seaweed cover was poor, except at the lower shore where encrusting reds were dominant. The shore appeared to be quite exposed from all southerly directions (SW - ESE). This may make it unsuitable for crabs and other large mobile spp. Cthamalus montagui and Corallina in the pools were characteristic as were Patella, Nucella and Topshells.

location_name: Lannacombe Bay (east) (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Corallina has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. The rock platform was dissected by gullies running mainly in a SW direction and contained shallow pools with sandy bottoms. Corallina was abundant in the rock pools. The top-mid beach area of incised rock had deep rock pools with cobbles and little else. Many large Thick Topshells , Dogwhelks and eggs were found to be present. Keyhole limpets and cowries were also found. The wide rocky platform extended in both directions from the beach . Mesophyllum lichenoide was found on the corrallina in the shore rockpools.

location_name: Great Sleaden Rock (Start Point to Little Sleadon Rock)
sample_comment: Pools dominated by corallinaceae with abundant Ceramium spp. and Patella ulyssiponensis. Most pools shallow, deeper pools with Laminaria digitata, Bifurcaria bifucata and Himanthalia elongata. Mytilus spat formed frequent patches in lower pools. Higher pools with few non coralline algae, dominated by coralline crusts and Patella ulyssiponensis.

location_name: Great Sleaden Rock (Start Point to Little Sleadon Rock)
sample_comment: Abundant vertical faces and overhangs of scoured surge gullies and rockmills were dominated by nearly 100% cover of Balanus perforatus. Actinia equina were usually present in large numbers and there was abundant coralline crusts. Halichondria panicea and Hymeniacidon perleve were common, and a variety of red algae, including Lomentaria articulata and Plumaria elegans were frequent.

A broad gully, 10m wide, with coarse sand on bedrock with overlying large slate boulders (to 1.5m). Algae attached to boulders or bedrocks beneath sand. Sand tolerant species in abundance, including Polyides and Furcellaria. Underboulder very rich with near 100% cover by sponges, didemnids and bryozoans, including Diplosoma spongiforme, Trididemnum cereum and Ophlitaspongia seriata. Decapoda well represented as were fish. A rich habitat.

location_name: Lannacombe Bay (west) (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Rhodophycota indet.(non-calc.crusts) has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Hard slate with igneous intrusions forming high (to 5m) ridges, with many overhangs and vertical faces. Exposed seaward sides had much Lithophyllum / Lithothamnion, Corallina spp, Barnacles, Actina equina and Nucella lapilus. On the sheltered sides there were many sponges (Halichondria, Hymeniacidon, Grantia) as well as Polysiphonia spp. and other filamentous reds. A large plateau extended beyond the ridges to LWST. Little rock was visible, being covered in encrusting red algae and Mastocarpus stellatus and kelp. The pools contained few boulders but there was an increased diversity of algae and fauna.

location_name: Lannacombe Bay (east) (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: A rugged bedrock plateau with numerous pools, gullies, overhangs and fissures. Restricted algal species and the majority of those present were not abundnant. Rockpools and gullies contained many boulders and some shelly gravel/sand. Actinia equina were abundant as were the corraline algae. In areas of heavy Patella grazing the Barnacles were dominant, particularly on the open, exposed faces. Richest areas for both species and abundance were the sheltered fissures and the vertical faces/overhangs.

location_name: Barler Rock (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Porifera indet crusts has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Whole area surveyed. Upper/midshore typical exposed species. Ls deep pools, overhangs and surge gullies very interesting with a diverse flora and fauna of less commonly faunal species such as Halurus equisetifolius, Asparagopsis armata and superabundant Tubularia indivisa in the surge gullies. An excellent site with more to be found on a better tide.

location_name: Lannacombe Beach West (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Porifera has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Rocky plateau with deep pools, fringed by Corallina, and Coralline crusts covering pool sides. Algae growth in pools sparse, often only on Patella. Deep gullies with vertical and overhanging sides harbouring many species in its fissured and creviced surface. Base of gullies scoured with sand/gravel and rock boulder base. More sheltered rockpools were rich in species and high in abundance. Anemones were common, particularly the beadlet. Sponges abundant. The survey reached MLWST, so only just into the Laminaria zone.

location_name: Gorah Rocks (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Ramalina siliquosa has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. One of a few rock outcrops which reach through the uppershore sand and shingle to reach the cliff base at 6-7m ACD. Lichen dominated though not particularly well covered, Ramalina siliquosa short and stubby. Green lichens occasional.

location_name: Great Sleaden Rock (Start Point to Little Sleadon Rock)
sample_comment: Ramalina siliquosa, Caloplaca has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. Supralittoral lichens were noted from over 40 metres above the shore, particularly Xanthoria parietina and Ramalina siliquosa, though only the 4-8m ASL band was surveyed. Abundance was high though diversity was not great. Grey lichens abundant.

location_name: Lannacombe (Langerstone Point to Prawle Point)
sample_comment: Exposed flat rocky shelf with many fissures and gullies. Rockpools formed in depressions. The shore is extremely exposed and this is reflected in the species found. Anemones were abundant as was coralline algae.

location_name: Langerstone Point (Langerstone Point to Prawle Point)
sample_comment: Supralittoral, predominatly upward facing bedrock, dominated by Caloplaca sp. Lecanora atra and indeterminate grey lickens. Ramalina sp. was abundant, particuarly growing out of fissures. There were several pools in this area, comepltely dominated by Enteromorpha.

location_name: Lannacombe Beach East (Harris Beach) (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Fucaceae, Nucella, Patella, Trochidae has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Chthamalus montagui has been given a nominal abundance value of Present for this record as in Arev it had no abundance value. The shore consisted of a fairly level, mica shist which contained many fissures and gullies, backed by mud cliffs. Rockpools had been formed in depressions on the surface. The area is characteristic for barnacle cover and encrusting red algae on drying and non-drying surfaces. Seaweed cover was poor, except at the lower shore where encrusting reds were dominant. The shore appeared to be quite exposed from all southerly directions (SW - ESE). This may make it unsuitable for crabs and other large mobile spp. Cthamalus montagui and Corallina in the pools were characteristic as were Patella, Nucella and Topshells.

location_name: Gara Rock Bay, East Portlemouth (Gammon Head to The Bull)
sample_comment: Lithophyllum has been removed from the species list for this record as more specific related taxa were also present, these are now marked as characterising. Bedrock of shists and igneous intrusions. Large rocky ridges and , near vertical faces with many overhangs, deep pools and fissures. Actina equina, Barnacles. Nucella lapillus and Patella vulgata were found on the exposed faces. Grantia compressa, Halichondria and Hymeniacion were found in shady places and overhangs with Plumaria elegans. Delesseria was found in shady places in deep pools. Opisthobranch were in fissures, as were many Cancer pagarus and Lipophrys pholis. Ridge adjacent to sandy bay and deep gully parallel to shoreline were surveyed.

location_name: Gorah Rocks (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)

Near 100% cover by Corallinacea, Actinia equina abundant as were Patella ulyssiponensis with Enteromorpha sp. attached. Bare rocks with sparse Fucus spiralis, Chthamalus and Littorina neritoides common. Ascophyllum nodosum appearing towards bottom of band.

location_name: Gorah Rocks (Little Sleadon Rock to Langerstone Point)
sample_comment: Slate and boulder filled gully just below shingle, running down the shore with through flowing water. Algae abundant, particularly Ceramium sp., Ulva and Chondrus etc. Sargassum in pools. Boulders with restricted underboulder community, relatively mobile and dominated by Spirorbidae. Corallinacea abundant, rock surfaces above water with abundant Fucoid and Ascophyllum growth.
